summary: Decision: Buy. Rationale: The 2Q18 earnings call (held shortly after the provided price history ending 2018-04-30) detailed record revenue of $61.1B (+16% YoY), EPS growth of 30%, six consecutive quarters of accelerating revenue growth, and robust expansion in Services (+31%) and Wearables (almost 50%). Crucially, management announced a new $100B share repurchase authorization and a 16% dividend increase, backed by a net cash position of $145B and strong free cash flow. Although 3Q18 revenue guidance of $51.5–53.5B is sequentially lower, this reflects normal seasonality rather than weakness. The past price data shows AAPL drifting from ~$41 in early March to $38.79 by April 30, indicating pre‑earnings caution and a depressed base. With the overwhelmingly positive results, massive capital return, and upbeat pipeline, the stock is poised to rise over the subsequent two months (by early July 2018). Therefore, the price two months after the earnings call date will exceed the call‑date closing level, favoring a Buy.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The 4Q19 earnings call revealed record September-quarter revenue of $64B (high end of expectations), accelerating Services growth (up 18% Y/Y to a record $12.5B), Wearables revenue growth well over 50%, and improved iPhone demand with the iPhone 11 becoming the best-selling model. The company guided 1Q20 revenue to $85.5–89.5B (a strong holiday quarter) and holds nearly $260B in cash plus marketable securities while actively repurchasing shares. The provided price history shows a clear uptrend from $50.12 on 2019-08-30 to $58.42 on 2019-10-29 (the last close before the late-October call) with healthy volumes, indicating positive market momentum. Given the robust fundamentals, optimistic guidance, and prevailing price trend, the stock is very likely to be trading above the earnings-call-date closing price in exactly two months (late December 2019). Therefore, buying now positions the position to profit before liquidation.

Decision: Sell. Rationale: The earnings call shows record 1Q16 results, but the forward guidance is weak: 2Q16 revenue is expected at only $50–53b versus $75.9b in 1Q16, implying a sharp sequential decline and likely a year‑over‑year drop amid severe currency headwinds, global macro slowdown, and early signs of softness in China. The past price data confirms a clear bearish trend into the call, with the stock falling from $26.51 on 2015‑11‑27 to $22.38 on 2016‑01‑25 on rising volume during down sessions. Given the disappointing outlook and the established downward momentum, the stock price two months after the call is likely to be lower than the call‑date close, so selling now is the prudent action.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The 3Q20 earnings call highlights record June-quarter revenue of $59.7B (+11% YoY), record Services revenue of $13.2B (+15% YoY), double-digit growth in iPad, Mac, and Wearables, and a net cash position of $81B with $10B of buybacks, demonstrating strong fundamental resilience amid COVID-19. The provided price history shows a consistent upward trend from $77.91 (Jun 1) to $92.02 (Jul 29) with healthy volume, indicating building market confidence ahead of the call. Combined with the positive results, broad geographic growth, and accelerating Services engagement, the stock is well-positioned to appreciate further. Therefore, two months after the earnings call (around end of September 2020), the price is likely to be higher than the call-date close, supporting a Buy decision.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: As a financial analyst reviewing the 3Q17 earnings call, I see overwhelmingly positive signals: revenue of $45.4B (up 7% YoY with accelerating growth for three straight quarters), record Services revenue ($7.3B, +22% YoY), double-digit growth in many markets, and strong performance across all product lines (iPhone, iPad, Mac, Watch, AirPods). The company provided upbeat 4Q17 guidance of $49–52B, indicating continued momentum. Additionally, the commentary on upcoming fall launches (iOS 11, ARKit, watchOS 4, HomePod) and a new Advanced Manufacturing Fund suggests further catalysts. The past price data shows the stock recovered from June lows near $33 to about $34.51 by July 31, reflecting improving sentiment ahead of the call. Given the robust fundamentals, positive guidance, and historical seasonality (new iPhone announcements typically boost shares in the following two months), I expect the stock price two months after the earnings call to be higher than the closing price on the call date. Therefore, I will buy now to profit at liquidation in two months.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The 1Q20 earnings call details record revenue of $91.8b (+9% YoY), record diluted EPS of $4.99, and broad-based growth across iPhone (+8%), Services (+17% to an all-time high), and Wearables (another record). The active installed base surpassed 1.5b, creating a durable engine for high-margin Services expansion. Management guided 2Q20 revenue of $63–67b, which reflects normal seasonality but still implies year-over-year growth, while a $10b share repurchase and $99b net cash position reinforce financial strength. The provided price history shows a clear upward trend from $64.36 (Nov 29) to $74.41 (Jan 27, used as a proxy for the call-date close) with only a minor pullback on rising volume, indicating constructive momentum. Given the combination of blowout fundamentals, continued capital returns, and positive price momentum, the stock is likely to trade higher two months after the call (by late March), so buying now maximizes expected proceeds at the two-month liquidation horizon.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The earnings call (held late April 2016, with the last pre-call close at $23.78 on Apr 25 and a similar level expected on the call date) revealed a quarterly revenue decline due to currency headwinds and tough comparisons, which the recent price data already reflects—the stock fell from $25.37 on Apr 14 to $23.78 on Apr 25. However, the transcript highlights strong underlying fundamentals: the active installed base surpassed 1 billion, services revenue jumped 20% to $6 billion, the purchase value of services tied to the installed base hit a record $9.9 billion (up 27%), Android switchers reached a record half-year high, India iPhone sales grew 56%, iPhone SE demand exceeds supply, and an updated capital return program was announced. These are clear forward-looking positives that suggest the market’s pre-earnings caution was overdone. Given the pullback and the encouraging outlook, I expect the stock to recover and trade above the earnings-call-date close within the two-month holding period, making a Buy the appropriate decision.

Decision: Buy. Strong Services momentum (goal to double in four years) and continued ecosystem expansion support a positive forward narrative. The 2Q17 revenue guidance of $51.5–53.5b is seasonally lower than Q1 but still implies stable-to-slightly-up year-over-year performance. The provided price history shows a steady uptrend from $25.43 (Nov 30) to $27.98 (Jan 30) with healthy volume, indicating building market confidence ahead of the print. Given the exceptional fundamentals, buyback support, and prevailing price momentum, I expect the stock to trade higher two months after the call (by late March 2017) than its closing price on the call date, making a Buy the correct decision for a position to be liquidated in exactly two months.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The earnings call reveals a fundamentally improving story beneath the headline iPhone decline: Services posted its best quarter ever ($11.5B revenue), paid subscriptions surged to over 390M, Wearables grew near 50%, and iPad revenue rose 22% YoY. The active installed base hit record levels, providing a durable foundation for future Services monetization. Management noted the iPhone revenue decline decelerated in the final weeks and saw early positive signs from pricing actions in China, suggesting the worst of the cyclical downturn may be passing. The company also announced a wave of new ecosystem services (Apple Card, Apple TV+, Arcade, News+) that reinforce customer lock‑in. Financially, the balance sheet is strong ($225B cash, $101B term debt) and $12B of buybacks were executed, signaling confidence. The past price data shows a clear upward trend from $41.26 (Feb 28) to $48.76 (Apr 29) into the call, reflecting accumulating bullish sentiment. Considering the positive tone, accelerating Services momentum, and prior price strength, I expect the stock to be higher two months after the earnings call date than on that date, making a Buy the appropriate decision for a position to be liquidated in two months.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The 4Q17 earnings call demonstrates overwhelmingly positive fundamentals: record September-quarter revenue of $52.6B (up 12% YoY), record diluted EPS of $2.07 (up 24% YoY), revenue growth across all product categories, and an all-time quarterly record for Services ($8.5B). Management guided 1Q18 revenue to $84–87B, explicitly expecting the biggest quarter ever due to strong iPhone 8/8 Plus and iPhone X demand, expanding Services, and double-digit growth in emerging markets. Given the bullish transcript, robust holiday-quarter guidance, and the established uptrend, I anticipate the shares will be higher two months from the earnings call date (early January 2018) than the ~$38.87 closing level. As a happy analyst with a constructive view, I predict appreciation by the two-month liquidation horizon and choose to buy.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The 4Q16 earnings call shows decisively strong fundamentals and momentum: revenue of $46.9b (high end of guidance), gross margin at the top of the 38% range, net income of $9b, EPS of $1.67, and record services revenue growing 24% YoY. iPhone demand is outstripping supply, Apple Pay transactions rose nearly 500% YoY, and the company issued upbeat 1Q17 revenue guidance of $76–78b, pointing to a robust holiday quarter. The firm also holds $237.6b in cash/marketable securities and is returning capital via buybacks. Given my upbeat mood and the favorable risk/reward, I will buy and liquidate in exactly two months.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The 3Q16 earnings call shows stronger-than-anticipated demand: revenue of $42.4b near the high end of guidance, gross margin of 38% at the top of range, and channel inventory reduced by $3.6b (more than the $2b expected), positioning the company well for coming months. iPhone sell-through improved, services hit a record $6b (up 19%), and the active installed base grew strongly. Management provided positive 4Q16 revenue guidance of $45.5–47.5b (above 3Q16) and expressed optimism on India and long-term China prospects, with new launches (iOS 10, Apple Pay on Safari) ahead. Combined with the upbeat fundamentals and guidance, the stock is likely to trade higher two months after the call, making a buy decision appropriate for a position to be liquidated in that horizon.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The 2Q17 earnings call reveals fundamentally strong results: revenue of $52.9b (near the high end of guidance), diluted EPS of $2.10 (up 10% YoY), gross margin of 38.9% (high‑end), and operating cash flow of $12.5b. Decision: Buy. Rationale: The earnings call reflects a strong 3Q19 with return to revenue growth, record Services revenue of $11.5B, Wearables growth well over 50%, narrowed iPhone decline, and constant-currency growth across all regions including a return to growth in Greater China. Decision: Buy. Rationale: The earnings call confirms that while iPhone revenue declined due to transitory factors (FX headwinds, reduced carrier subsidies, battery replacement program, and acute macro weakness in Greater China), the underlying business remains robust: Services set an all-time record ($10.9B, +19% YoY), wearables grew over 50% in China, the active installed base reached a new high of 1.4B, and non-iPhone revenue grew 19% outside of China. The company also returned $8.2B via buybacks and holds $245B in cash/marketable securities, signaling financial strength and management confidence. The price data shows the stock already absorbed the January guidance cut (drop to $33.74) and recovered to ~$37 by late January, establishing an upward recovery trend. With the call providing clarity and emphasizing long-term growth drivers, investor sentiment should improve over the next two months. Thus, the closing price around the call date (~$37) is likely to be lower than the price two months later, favoring a Buy.

Decision: Buy. Decision: Buy. Rationale: The 1Q18 earnings call details an exceptionally strong quarter: record revenue ($88.3b, +13% YoY and +21% on a per‑week basis), record diluted EPS ($3.89), record net income ($20.1b), and a high‑end gross margin of 38.4%. Growth was broad‑based across geographies and products, with the active installed base hitting 1.3b, Services revenue up 18% YoY, paid subscriptions surpassing 240m, and iPhone ASP rising to $796 on the success of iPhone X and 8/8 Plus. The past price data shows the stock drifting from about $41.90 in mid‑January to $39.14 by Jan 31 – a pullback that likely reflected pre‑earnings caution rather than fundamental weakness. With the confirmed record results and upbeat guidance, the market should re‑rate the stock over the next two months. Given the position will be liquidated in exactly two months, I expect the price to be above the earnings‑call‑date close (approximately the last available $39.14 level, or any slightly higher close on the call day) as the positive fundamentals are digested. Therefore, the probabilistic edge is to the upside and the appropriate action is to Buy.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The earnings call transcript paints an exceptionally strong picture of Apple’s fiscal Q4 2018: record September-quarter revenue of $62.9B (+20% YoY), record EPS growth of 41%, and all-time highs in iPhone, Services, Mac, and Wearables segments. Decision: Buy. Rationale: The 2Q20 earnings call reveals that Apple grew revenue to $58.3b (+1% YoY) despite the COVID-19 shock, set all-time records in Services and quarterly records in Wearables, and delivered EPS of $2.55. The company repurchased $18.5b of stock, signalling management confidence, and ended the quarter with $83b net cash. Operationally, supply chains normalized by late March and the transcript highlights surging demand for iPads (e.g., 350k units to NYC schools) and Apple Watch health features, showing the ecosystem’s renewed importance in remote work, education, and telemedicine. The price history shows the stock fell to $54.16 on Mar 23 amid market panic but recovered to $69.46 by Apr 29, a strong V-shaped recovery and positive momentum heading into the call. While near-term guidance was withdrawn, the fundamental resilience, continued buybacks, and upward price trend suggest further appreciation as economies gradually reopen over the next two months. Using the last pre-call close (~$69.46) as the earnings-call-day reference, I expect the stock to trade higher by late June 2020 when the position is liquidated. My good mood aligns with the constructive signals, but the decision is anchored in the transcript’s strength and the price recovery pattern.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The Q4 2018 earnings call highlights strong fundamentals: 23% full-year revenue growth, expanded gross margins to the most profitable level since 2011, and 65% of Q4 sales from new high-performance products. Ryzen client CPU unit share rose for the fifth straight quarter, EPYC server share reached mid-single digits with sequential doubling, and data center GPUs secured major wins (e.g., Google Project Stream). Management expressed confidence in 2019 launches (30% more Ryzen systems, 50% more notebooks) and improving channel inventory. Past price data shows the stock rebounded from a $16.65 December low to ~$20.18 on Jan 28, with a slight pre-call dip that likely reflects caution rather than weakness. Given the positive trajectory, product momentum, and upcoming Feb/March investor conferences, the stock is poised to appreciate over the next two months, making its price two months after the call higher than on the call date.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The Q4 2016 earnings call reveals a clear turnaround at AMD: revenue grew 15% YoY to $1.11B, computing and graphics segment revenue hit a 2‑year high, client and GPU revenues were the strongest in many quarters, and the company achieved full‑year non‑GAAP operating profitability with positive free cash flow while reducing debt. Crucially, the call confirms Ryzen desktop processors are on track for an early March launch (within the 2‑month holding window) with broad OEM and channel support, and the server CPU “Naples” is generating strong design‑win momentum. The past price data shows the stock rose from $8.91 (Nov 30) to ~$10.61 (Jan 30) with a healthy pullback from December peaks, suggesting consolidation ahead of catalysts. Given the robust fundamentals, imminent product launches, and positive market sentiment, the stock price two months after the call (post‑Ryzen launch) should be higher than the call‑date level, making a buy decision appropriate.

Decision: Buy. Rationale: The Q1 2018 earnings call shows strong fundamental improvement: revenue of $1.65B, up 40% year-over-year, gross margin +4 percentage points, and significantly higher EPS. The Computing and Graphics segment delivered double-digit growth, with Ryzen processors now 60% of client revenue (up from low 40s) and expanding OEM adoption (25 new Ryzen notebooks expected in Q2, 60 by year-end). Graphics revenue benefited from gaming and blockchain demand, with supply ramping. Past price data indicates the stock pulled back from $12.42 in late February to a close of $10.09 on the call date (April 24, 2018) but stabilized and ticked up in April, suggesting oversold conditions. Given consistent execution, improving mix, and positive product momentum, the stock is likely to trade above $10.09 by the two-month horizon (≈June 24, 2018), making a Buy the appropriate decision before liquidation.

Decision: Buy. Decision: Buy. Decision: Buy. Decision: Buy. Rationale: The Q2 2018 earnings call demonstrates strong fundamental health: revenue grew 53% year-over-year to $1.76B, gross margin expanded over 3 points, and AMD posted its highest quarterly net income in 7 years. Both business segments showed robust growth, with Ryzen mobile shipments more than doubling sequentially, all three major OEMs adopting Ryzen PRO, and EPYC server processors achieving >50% sequential revenue/unit growth with 50+ platforms. Decision: Buy. Rationale: The Q3 2017 earnings call transcript reflects a strong quarter with revenue up 26% YoY to $1.64B, improved gross margins, profitability, and positive free cash flow. Decision: Buy. Decision: Buy. Decision: Sell. Rationale: The earnings call reveals that while AMD’s computing and graphics segment showed sequential growth and promising long-term catalysts (Polaris GPUs, Zen CPUs, VR momentum), the company explicitly guided to a seasonally weaker first half and ongoing macro challenges in China. Q4 total revenue still fell 10% sequentially to $958M, and the truly meaningful product launches (Polaris mid-2016, new semi-custom revenue in H2, Zen sampling later in 2016) fall outside the two-month liquidation window. The past price data shows a clear bearish trend: after peaking at $3.00 in late December, the stock slid to $2.03 by Jan 15 on rising volume, indicating sustained selling pressure.
